text:
What the market is mispricing on $CRDO

Did you know $CRDO is the only company that sells the entire interconnect stack - chip, cable, transceiver, photonics, software?

That’s the moat.💥

Bears point to gross margin compression after the $750M DustPhotonics acquisition. They’re missing the trade.

What they bought:
• Silicon Photonics PIC tech in-house
• $500M+ optical revenue path by FY27
• $6B SiPho TAM by 2030
• EPS-accretive in FY27
• Vertical integration into the optical engine

Peer margins (latest reported):
$AAOI: 31%
$LITE: 39%
$CIEN: 43%
$CRDO: 68.6%

$CRDO margin is 25+ points higher than every pure-play optical competitor. 

They’re not commoditizing - they’re consolidating.

The 5 stack:
🔌 Copper AECs - no real competitor at 1.6T
⚡ DSPs + proprietary SerDes IP
💡 ZeroFlap optical transceivers
🔬 Silicon Photonics - now in-house
🧠 PILOT predictive telemetry

*No one else owns all five layers*

Meanwhile their 1.6T ZeroFlap AECs are fully cabling $NVDA flagship Kyber Ultra NVL576 - the most important AI rack of the next 24 months.

Market sees: “expensive stock, margin compression.”

Market is missing: vertical integration into a $700B AI capex cycle, with the only company that owns the entire physical layer.

The market is mispricing the moat. 🚀

Long $CRDO

2) Key photonics tech for AI: 🧑‍🔧

• High-speed lasers & modulators

• Silicon photonics integration

• Co-packaged optics (CPO)

• Advanced laser arrays for massive parallelism

These enable the interconnects that make giant GPU clusters for agentic workloads possible. China is pouring resources into domestic supply while still tapping foreign suppliers.

text:
The 4-Layer Photonics Stack Powering Tomorrow’s AI Clusters  Gemini Trading just released this clean, layered infographic that maps exactly how photonics is replacing copper in AI infrastructure — from silicon to global cloud.  Layer 1 – Chips
Electrical side: DSP + Driver + TIA.
Photonic side: Laser + Modulator + Photodetector. The foundation.  Layer 2 – Photonic Engine & Module
These chips combine into a full Photonic Engine, then packaged as an Optical Module — turning electrical signals into light.  Layer 3 – Packaging Options
• Traditional Pluggable modules
• Near-Packaged Optics (NPO)
• Game-changing Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) — placing optics right beside the switch ASIC for minimal latency and power loss.  Layer 4 – Distances & Network
Light travels across every scale:  Within rack  
Rack-to-rack  
Row/floor  
Data center to data center

Underpinning everything: advanced Material Platforms — InP, Silicon Photonics PIC, and TFLN modulators — each optimized for speed, power, and cost.  Timeline? Expect major deployment acceleration in +6 months and full maturity by +1 year.  This isn’t incremental — it’s the architectural shift hyperscalers need to keep training trillion-parameter models without melting data centers. CPO + silicon photonics = dramatically lower power, higher bandwidth, and sustainable AI scaling.  At Gemini Trading we track these breakthroughs because they don’t just move data — they move markets.  Who’s winning the photonics race? Drop your take below  and tag a fellow investor!  @GeminiTrading

text:
The follow-up nobody’s posting on $CRDO:

April 13: They acquired DustPhotonics for $750M.

Now they own the ENTIRE stack:

🔌 Copper AECs (no competitor)

💡 Optical DSPs + Transceivers

🔬 Silicon Photonics (in-house)

Result: $500M+ optical revenue by FY27. 

EPS-accretive. $6B SiPho TAM.

Meanwhile their 1.6T AECs fully cable Nvidia’s flagship Kyber Ultra NVL576 rack.

Market sees: “margin compression.”

Market is missing: vertical integration across a $700B AI capex cycle.

Valuation risk ≠ business risk.

The setup is asymmetric because the business isn’t broken. The multiple is just inflated.

That’s the thesis. Long $CRDO 📈

text:
The eight names occupy materially different positions in the CPO stack, from upstream substrates (AXTI) to laser devices (SIVEF, AAOI), specialty materials (LWLG), photonic platforms (POET, HIMX), DSP/silicon photonics (CRDO post-DustPhotonics), and module/CPO assembly (FN, AAOI). 

The single most important framing for a thesis-driven CPO investor: roughly 70% of the dollars in this complex are still being driven by the 1.6T pluggable cycle, not co-packaged architectures. 

The names whose stock prices are most levered to pluggable volumes (AAOI, and to a lesser degree FN’s datacom segment) are also the names most exposed to CPO if it inflects faster than consensus expects in 2027. 

The names that win in both scenarios are CRDO (hybrid via DustPhotonics SiPho acquisition) and the InP upstream (AXTI/SIVEF), since lasers feed CPO modules at higher unit content than they fed pluggables.

the $LYTE ETF is mega-bullish As I mentioned before, photonics is super unknown to retail investors, so you understand The ~ % of people who know about companies in the sector: Semiconductors ~ 85/95% $NVDA $TSMC $AMD Memory ~ 55/70% $SNDK $MU Samsung Photonics/CPO ~ 5/10% $SIVE $AAOI $COHR Now that you understand how unknown photonics is, think for a moment If when the $DRAM ETF came out we saw a huge rally in the memory sector What is supposed to happen when this Photonics ETF becomes a reality If memory is much better known and there was already a ton of flow coming from retail investors who didn't have the knowledge or the guts to buy individual stocks in the sector Then here, where much fewer people know about photonics, two things are going to happen IMO; - Since people know less about it, less capital will come in compared to $DRAM, but the sector is tiny compared to memory So even if it's less capital, it will have a much bigger impact and everything will shoot up vertically - The other thing is that this would be a massive showcase for the entire sector, as people will discover companies they didn't even know existed That translates into more and more flow coming in This is Photonics stocks already massively rerated.

That’s why I’d rather own select names I researched deeply than blindly buy the entire basket.

But the ETF angle still matters, most retail investors barely know what photonics are.

This would ultimately brings additional capital into the entire sector.

text:
Here’s my long-winded post on $SIVE for the weekend.

Next week is bloody important. ER hits, MSCI inclusion soon after. 

$SIVE Catalysts:

Sivers Semiconductors is a Swedish leader in photonics (high-power lasers for AI data centers/co-packaged optics) and wireless/mmWave tech (5G/6G, SATCOM, beamformers). 

The company is riding multiple tailwinds in AI infrastructure, defense, and transatlantic tech cooperation.

Key Recent & Upcoming Catalysts:

1. CHIPS Act Funding (US Strategic Validation)
•  Sivers has secured awards through the Northeast Microelectronics Coalition (NEMC) Hub under the US CHIPS and Science Act (Microelectronics Commons program).
•  Awards include ~$6M+ initial funding for 5G/6G beamformer ICs/array design (FR3 frequencies) and Electronic Warfare projects, with potential to reach ~$11.6M+ in year 1 and up to ~$30M over multiple years if extended. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 
•  Partners include major players like Ericsson, Raytheon, BAE Systems, MIT, and others. This de-risks development, funds US-aligned prototyping, and strengthens Sivers’ position in secure supply chains for defense and advanced comms.

2. White House / US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal
•  Recent signing of the US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal (highlighted in White House releases and statements by Secretary of State Rubio) deepens cooperation in AI, 5G/6G, biotech, quantum, defense tech, advanced manufacturing, and trusted infrastructure. 
•  This bilateral push aligns perfectly with Sivers’ portfolio in AI optics, lasers, photonics, and wireless tech. It signals stronger US-Sweden tech ties, potential for more collaboration/funding, and elevated strategic importance for Swedish innovators like Sivers. 

3. Nasdaq Dual Listing Evaluation
•  In April 2026, Sivers announced it’s evaluating a dual listing on Nasdaq New York (while keeping Swedish domicile). This aims to tap deeper US capital markets, broaden the investor base, and support growth in AI/photonics. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 
•  Preparations include audit uplifts to US standards (PCAOB). Positive for liquidity, visibility, and valuation re-rating as US investors gain easier access.

4. Other Strong Tailwinds:
•  AI Optics Boom: High-power lasers for co-packaged optics in next-gen AI data centers. Partnerships (e.g., O-Net, POET) targeting volume production in late 2026. Strong demand with structural shortages noted by peers. @i 
•  Pipeline growth: ~$453M (up 64% YoY), product revenue mix improving significantly. @hoarderresearch 
•  Q1 results expected May 29, 2026 – potential positive print catalyst.
•  Index inclusions and overall sector momentum in AI/semiconductors.
$SIVE has seen sharp moves recently on AI/photonics hype, but remains a smaller-cap story with real execution and policy support behind it. The combination of CHIPS funding, US-Sweden tech pact, Nasdaq ambitions, and AI optics exposure creates a compelling multi-catalyst setup.
Not financial advice – DYOR. Markets are volatile, especially in small-cap tech/semiconductors.

What are your thoughts on $SIVE? 

Holding or watching? 🚀

text:
Then comes the real late-cycle signal: productization.

$DRAM, $EUV, $LYTE and other narrow AI-infrastructure ETFs for memory, photonics, etc. are now showing up. Leveraged versions and filings are following too.

First the stocks run.
Then the ETF wrappers come.
Then leveraged retail products arrive.

Historically, that is not early-cycle behavior. This is late-cycle behavior. 

9/12

text: Genuine shortage, yes. But the bottleneck keeps moving. Power was the ceiling, compute, now memory has lead times stretching to a year and photonics components are next. Each time one constraint loosens another appears. not necessarily a bubble indicator but it does complicate the 'build it and they'll pay' thesis.

Market remains choppy. Green open in the morning, sharp dip during the post-lunch session followed by an end-of-day recovery — showing traders still buying dips but not fully committing yet. Semiconductors, photonics and AI infrastructure names are seeing profit taking after strong runs, while software is finally showing some bounce and rotation flow. Wouldn’t be surprised to see a few more volatile sideways sessions before the market decides its next major directional move. Patience and risk management matter most in this environment.

text:
If you are down big today on semiconductors and AI names like $SIVE or $ALMU or $BRUN or any of them, remember to zoom out. 

1. The capex for AI is about to more than double in 5 years

2. Photonics and especially CPO will take a disproportional 10x rise. So will hyperscaler infra stocks

3. All these stocks are already up 100-200% in 1-2 months. They dipped 10-20-30%

If you are buying and holding long term to see through the thesis which amounts to remaking the world's economy, you don't have much to worry about.

Goldman Sachs projects annual AI capex of $765 billion in 2026, growing to $1.6 trillion by 2031. 

2026’s figure includes $494 billion of capex for compute and $232 billion for data centers, scaling to $1.13 trillion for compute and $436 billion for data centers by 2031.

💰Vancouver-based quantum computing company Photonics
has raised over $200 million in a fresh funding round led by Planet First Partners.
https://t.co/mi1pUVya3w… 
The round saw participation from a mix of new and existing investors, including BDC, Export Development Canada, Bell Ventures, and Mubadala Capital. Microsoft, RBC, and TELUS had also come on board.

text:
.@NSF just committed $1.5B over a decade to X-Labs: independent teams funded on milestones, not papers, to push hardware from prototype to commercial platform.

Both opening tracks are #quantum: sensing-driven instruments, and interconnects/integrated photonics for linking heterogeneous systems.

The bet here is structural, not just financial. Federal money built to de-risk the translation gap before private capital arrives.

https://t.co/3BnIPFEFda

text:
Opened new positions recently in:

AAOI
APH
EQT
GEV
GLW
POWL

Optics and power. I was late to optics/photonics. I was right on time with some power names like ETN PWR VRT.

The power layer of the AI capex stack is thick and deserves its own substack so I’m filling in gaps with fuel, conditioning, interconnect and generation.

Looking into storage tomorrow.

Power is the bottleneck. Many names have moved but many are in nice pullbacks or have yet to move.

I’m holding all my pure AI names and adding more AI power names.

I’m also adding to pure AI on dips.

I sold defense but might get back in but I’m thinking to just focus on AI only but AI touches everything (eventually) including defense, robotics, healthcare, finance, consumer etc. It’s just a matter of timing.

text:
$VIAV saw some big call buying this morning near the lows. $500k bet on sept $49 strike calls. One of my top 10 positions but not a top 5 position. Trading at roughly 39x 2027 estimates currently, which is LOW compared a lot of the photonics/cpo related stocks I track. I did add just a couple of March 2027 calls on this pullback but am being very cautious overall to go too big too soon. 

What are your favorite CPO/Photonics related stocks are current prices?

text:
Photonics System Logic — How AI Clusters Actually Scale  Gemini Trading just dropped this crystal-clear infographic breaking down the entire photonics stack powering modern AI infrastructure.  It starts simple:
Servers & AI Accelerators feed into an Electrical chip via high-speed SerDes links. But copper has limits. That’s where Packaging Options take over:  • Pluggable optics (traditional modules)
• Near-Packaged Optics (NPO)
• Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) — the hottest new architecture  Each packaging choice pairs with advanced Material Platforms: Indium Phosphide (InP), Silicon Photonics PIC, and TFLN modulators — all optimized for different performance, power, and cost profiles.  The magic? These optical engines then connect everything across real-world distances:  Within rack (0–2 m)  
Rack-to-rack (2–100 m)  
Row / floor (100 m–2 km)

…all the way to massive Optical Fabric / Cloud scale.  This isn’t theory — it’s the exact blueprint hyperscalers and chip giants are racing to deploy. CPO and silicon photonics are slashing power consumption, crushing latency, and enabling the insane bandwidth AI training demands.  From electrical signals to light-speed optical interconnects, the diagram shows why photonics is the only way trillion-parameter models can keep growing without melting data centers.  At Gemini Trading we track these breakthroughs because they don’t just move bits — they move markets.  Who’s leading the charge in 2026? Drop your take below  and tag a fellow tech investor!  @GeminiTrading

text:
Yeah seems like a multi year bull cycle and most retail and investors aren't even in yet if you're talking about semi's, photonics, networking, small/mid cap speculative energy plays around AI.

I think we get a decent 5-7% dip eventually, but rebound harder. Mid terms are coming up so might not be until after that in October, nobody knows for certain. The names listed above are literally holding up the entire market though, so I'm pretty sure Trump and Fed will do everything in their power to keep the train running.

Interestingly enough $TSM appears to be a bottleneck on chips and apparently is the thing that's going to stop this from being a full blown bubble, and this will play out the right way for a few years.

Exciting time to be investing for sure. Market will probably pump harder if the 🇺🇸 and Iran come to an agreement soon next week.

text:
Decent day, managed to get into $MXL and $COHR. 

Had early positioning in $AAOI before April run but sold that off below the 20 the other day and sought exposure in the purer photonics’ names per my research. LITE snuck away too fast and didn’t get my fills, but COHR stuck. 

I still think there is a blowoff in the cards for a lot of the stocks out there right now, and will continue to actively position in those that I think have the potential for big, exhaustive moves. 

If I were you I would be going back and practice trading parabolas from history, tons of lessons in how to hold for the big move and not get trimmed into oblivious trying to protect what you don’t understand.

• $IBM: $1 billion
• $GFS: $375 million
• $QBTS, $RGTI, and Infleqtion: ~$100 million each
• Diraq: $38 million

text: Thank you to the @NorthwesternU student section of the SPIE (the International Society of Optics and Photonics) for inviting me to speak on our work in the area of advanced optical systems for biomedical devices – from cellular-scale LEDs with wireless power and control for optogenetic neuroscience studies using small animal models, to wearable microfluidic ‘lab on the skin’ devices with colorimetric readout of chemical species in eccrine sweat for sports, worker safety and patient care. For live demos and show/tell, I handed around some of the optogenetic devices, now available from our startup company @NeuroLuxInc, for activation using the NFC functionality on most smartphones, and I gave out two Gx patches, joint product offering through @Gatorade and our startup company @epicorebio , to the first two students who asked questions after the talk. The venue was an interesting room in a beautiful Gothic-style building that was originally part of the Seabury-Western Theological Seminary, an Episcopal seminary, now part of Northwestern University – first time for me to be in this building, which also serves as The Graduate Student Commons. Very unusual - two levels of seating, including couches and desks, all with an advanced AV system for the Zoom broadcast of the presentation. Great energy from a group of really smart students! Thanks to Ramin Yazdaanpanah @raminyazdaaa, Komal Prasad and Qin Tong Wu of the student chapter for inviting me and for organizing the event!

text:
$SIVE

The White House just put US-Sweden cooperation around AI, 5G/6G, cloud, electronics, defense tech, advanced manufacturing and trusted infrastructure on paper.

That matters.

Because this is no longer just a “small Swedish photonics story.”

This is starting to become a strategic infrastructure story.

Sivers is exposed to the exact layers the US and Sweden now want to deepen:

AI data center optics
laser sources
advanced connectivity
defense-grade photonics
trusted tech supply chains
US capital market optionality

The market may still see $SIVE as a tiny Nordic semiconductor name.

I think that is the opportunity.

Photonic chokepoints are moving from niche to national strategy.

text:
$SIVE Sivers Semiconductors: Strategic Validation Across AI Photonics, RF and Defense Infrastructure

https://t.co/EnmkiWUSEN

This $6.6M Year 2 EW STAR award is a very important signal, in my view.

The contract value itself is not the main point. The main point is continued validation from within the U.S. defense microelectronics ecosystem.

Sivers is working alongside BAE Systems, MIT Lincoln Laboratory and Columbia University on next-generation electronic warfare, radar and advanced communications architectures. For a small-cap semiconductor company, that is a serious credibility marker.

What I like most is how the Sivers story is broadening.

This is not only AI/datacenter photonics.

It is AI photonics plus InP lasers, SATCOM/LEO connectivity, advanced RF, beamforming, defense communications and potential U.S. institutional access.

That combination can become extremely powerful if execution continues.

Many semiconductor companies have one promising vertical. Sivers appears to be building relevance across several strategic markets at the same time — each tied to bandwidth, latency, power efficiency, secure connectivity and national security.

That is why I believe the market may still be far from fully pricing in the strategic value of this company.

The award is not just funding.

It is validation that Sivers is being pulled deeper into critical infrastructure layers where specialized semiconductor technology matters.

The contract value itself is not the main point. The main point is continued validation from within the U.S. defense microelectronics ecosystem. Sivers is working alongside BAE Systems, MIT Lincoln Laboratory and Columbia University on next-generation electronic warfare, radar and advanced communications architectures. For a small-cap semiconductor company, that is a serious credibility marker. What I like most is how the Sivers story is broadening. This is not only AI/datacenter photonics. But the real shocker in this Morgan Stanley slide is actually power, because the industry is now talking about moving from roughly 120kW per rack today toward potentially 600kW per rack by the Vera Rubin Ultra generation in 2027, which is an almost unimaginable escalation in power density within an incredibly short period of time.

To put this into perspective, many traditional enterprise datacenters historically operated at only a few kilowatts per rack, while even modern hyperscale campuses today often consume only tens of megawatts in total facility power draw. But once you begin deploying hundreds or thousands of 600kW AI racks simultaneously, the math becomes almost absurd because a large-scale Vera Rubin Ultra cluster could eventually consume gigawatts of electricity, effectively rivaling the energy demand of a mid-sized city.

And this is where the market still massively underestimates the second-order implications of the AI boom, because the bottleneck is no longer simply semiconductors, GPUs, or memory supply. The bottleneck increasingly becomes electricity itself.

The US power grid can barely keep up with current AI infrastructure demand already, while transmission congestion, transformer shortages, substation constraints, cooling limitations, permitting bottlenecks, and aging grid infrastructure are becoming increasingly visible across major datacenter hubs. Importantly, grid infrastructure cannot scale at semiconductor speed. You can accelerate chip production with enough capital expenditure and engineering talent, but building transmission lines, substations, generation capacity, cooling systems, and interconnection approvals often requires many years due to environmental reviews, local opposition, labor shortages, and physical construction constraints.

This is precisely why we continue believing the AI buildout is not a two-to-three-year investment cycle, but instead a decade-long industrial transformation that increasingly resembles the buildout of railroads, electricity networks, and telecom infrastructure during previous industrial revolutions.

And this is also why energy infrastructure is quietly becoming one of the most important and underappreciated AI trades globally.

The winners are no longer just GPU companies. The winners increasingly include utilities like Constellation Energy and Vistra, nuclear-related plays like Oklo and NuScale Power, gas infrastructure companies like Kinder Morgan and Williams Companies, grid and electrical equipment suppliers like GE Vernova, Eaton, Schneider Electric, and Vertiv, as well as transformer, cooling, and datacenter infrastructure providers that now sit directly inside the physical backbone required to support next-generation compute.

Hyperscalers themselves are starting to understand this reality. Companies like Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are no longer simply software companies buying servers. They are increasingly becoming quasi-energy infrastructure companies because securing long-duration power availability is becoming strategically inseparable from securing compute capacity itself.

That is why nuclear power is quietly returning to the center of the conversation. Hyperscalers may eventually fund or directly partner on nuclear generation projects out of pure necessity because renewable intermittency alone cannot reliably support ultra-high-density AI clusters operating continuously at scale.

In many ways, AI is beginning to collide with physical reality. You cannot run trillion-dollar next-generation compute infrastructure on transmission systems and grid architectures that were largely built decades ago for a completely different industrial era.

The semiconductor story may have started the AI race, but energy infrastructure may ultimately determine who wins it."}}}, "rest_id": "2057723120103170197", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "172929", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2057899801945973187", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "915", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
INTERVIEW ON CPO: Radha Nagarajan, the Senior Vice President and Chief Technology Officer of Marvell’s Optical Engineering Group. 

Q: At what point do you think silicon photonics will take over discrete components/modules?

I would say silicon photonics has already taken over to a large extent, especially at 100 G and higher data rates. Yield is not something unique to just silicon photonics—it is a critical requirement for any practical technology. However, what silicon photonics uniquely enables is particularly important for the industry. First, it allows companies without their own foundry, like Marvell, to design and manufacture highly complex photonic components by leveraging the existing semiconductor infrastructure. Second, it provides true scalability. Marvell, for example, has been one of the well-known companies implementing silicon photonics for applications between datacenters. Intel pioneered silicon photonics for applications inside the datacenters. Today, many of the large transceiver companies have mostly transitioned to silicon photonics. Given this level of adoption, it is fair to say that silicon photonics has already ‘taken over’ in many key segments of the optical interconnect market.

Q: Many silicon photonics systems still rely on external laser packaging. Do you see integrated light sources becoming mainstream, or will off-chip lasers dominate for practical reasons?

If you look at the integrated laser work published over the years, including our early demonstrations, Intel’s results, and developments at Infinera (now Nokia), it is clear that integrating light sources heterogeneously on silicon or monolithically in InP is commercially viable. The real obstacle is not technical capability, but perception—many people still assume that integrated lasers will suffer from reliability issues. One concern stems from the behavior of indium phosphide lasers at elevated temperatures. It is true that performance can degrade at high temperatures, but when operated around a base temperature of roughly 70–75 °C, these lasers can already operate perfectly well. In fact, Intel has demonstrated strong reliability in this regime. The key is careful design. Temperature variations can be significant, and the resulting wavelength shifts must be properly managed. In practice, engineers have already developed effective strategies to handle these challenges. So, from a technical perspective, I do not see major roadblocks. The bigger challenge may be changing people’s mindset.

Let me also make a prediction. Cooling large-scale server infrastructure is becoming increasingly challenging. The industry is already transitioning from air cooling to liquid cooling, and some are exploring immersion cooling. These approaches can provide more stable and potentially lower operating temperatures, which could create an opportunity for integrated lasers as well.

Q: How do you see the potential of heterogeneous integration?

I think silicon photonics, as a platform for integration, has enormous potential. What we’ve achieved so far is only scratching the surface. There are multiple paths forward here. You can integrate different materials, combine multiple types of devices within the same material platform, or even move toward stacking multiple device layers. All of these approaches are actively being explored, and I believe many of them will enter production in the near future. As data rates continue to increase, heterogeneous integration will also involve incorporating new higher performance materials, opening up more possibilities. At the same time, integration itself is becoming more complex. We’re moving beyond traditional 2D integration. People are now talking about 2.5D, 3D, even 3.5D integration, which will create many new opportunities for system design and performance improvement. 

Q: You were one of the early voices pushing co-packaged optics (CPO) for AI systems, now more and more companies are doing this. What convinced you CPO was not just a research direction, but something the industry would truly need?

The reason I have advocated for CPO stems from my firm belief that heterogeneous integration is the key to high-speed applications. During my time at Inphi (now at Marvell), we were already seeing the limits of traditional approaches. As data rates continued to increase, techniques like wire bonds simply could not keep up. The logical next step was to bring electronics and photonics closer through heterogeneous integration. That was the original motivation—overcoming speed and bandwidth bottlenecks. But as we developed these approaches, it became clear that they also align very well with what CPO is trying to achieve. Over time, the value of co-packaging has expanded beyond just pushing the highest data rates. We now see it being applied even at relatively lower data rates, especially in large-scale AI systems. Whether you look at scale-up, scale-out, or scale-across systems, they all benefit from tightly integrated photonics with electronics. So, in a way, heterogeneous integration is the essence, and CPO is one of its most important applications.

Q: Speed is the reason that drives us from ‘pluggable’ to ‘on board’. Many believe ‘pluggable’ can be extended with better DSPs and packaging, while others argue they won’t scale forever’. From your perspective, will this ‘extension strategy’ scale indefinitely? If you had to point to a single limiting factor, what would ultimately block ‘pluggable’ from keeping up with 100 T, or 200 T switch ASICs?

In my opinion, with 100 T or 200 T switches and systems at the horizon, you will need co-packaging. It’s true that the industry is very comfortable with ‘pluggable’ solutions, and there is a strong incentive to extend their life as much as possible. In many scale-up systems, copper will continue to be used as long as it makes sense from a power and cost perspective. For the same reasons, data center operators would naturally prefer to stay with pluggable solutions.

However, when developing new products, you have to look ahead. Since late last year, analysts have begun including CPO in their revenue projections, which signals a real shift in the industry. In fact, data center operators and hyperscalers are now actively funding CPO developments. That tells you this is no longer just a research direction. Technically, pluggables can be pushed further, even beyond 200 G per lane, but the cost is power. As you scale bandwidth, the power required for high-speed electrical interfaces and DSPs becomes increasingly difficult to manage.

This is where CPO becomes very attractive, especially for scale-up architecture like GPU or accelerator interconnects. I actually think the first major deployment for CPO will happen in scale-up systems, and then expand into scale-out.

We’re already seeing strong signals from the ecosystem. Nvidia has demonstrated multiple generations of CPO platforms, including at GTC and ISSCC. From a foundry perspective, this aligns very well with TSMC’s roadmap. The idea of tightly coupling electrical compute with optical I/O is no longer a dream—it could be just a year or two away.

That said, predicting the exact timing is always difficult. It’s much easier to explain the past than to forecast the future. When you work at the intersection of technology development and product deployment, there is always uncertainty about when the market will be ready. In many ways, developing new technologies can feel like placing a bet—and that’s simply part of innovation.

Q: People often say that co-packaging is always the difficult part, what do you think about that?

That perception largely comes from how optical packaging used to be done. If you compare it to electrical packaging, the difference is quite clear. Electrical packaging has been highly automated for a long time. But if you look at the optical packaging in early days, you’ll find yourself doing things one at a time, wire bonding electronics and manually aligning fibers, which are slow and not scalable. But once volume kicks in, things start to change. A lot of these processes are now highly automated. There are still operators involved, but the work is largely handled by automatic equipment. Efficient fiber coupling is still a challenge, no question. But there are already solutions out there, and they’re getting better. As volume continues to ramp, I believe the fiber coupling part will gradually be automated as well. If you look at where we are today, photonics manufacturing is already very different from five years ago, and nothing like ten years ago. And honestly, things are only going to move faster from here.

Q: Are Through-Silicon Via (TSVs) essential for CPO?

We demonstrated and published extensively on TSV as an option way to integrate electronics on top and then route down into silicon photonics bottom layer. But you don’t have to always do that. You can flip the architecture and put the optics on top instead, and some groups are actively exploring that. One reason people hesitate is thermal. Electronics tend to run hotter than optics, so it’s often easier to heat-sink from the top, if the electronics are there. But that’s not a fundamental limitation—there are many alternative thermal management strategies people are exploring as well.

There are also practical considerations in the fab that may lead to a preference for one configuration over another. Early on, there was a strong tendency to adopt TSV-based approach. For example, you now have packaging techniques like fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP), which does not require TSVs at all. Several companies are already offering this technology, and some are even beginning to deploy CPO based on it.

So, the absence of TSVs is not a fundamental roadblock—there are multiple viable ways to implement these systems.

Q: At what point do you think silicon photonics will take over discrete components/modules? I would say silicon photonics has already taken over to a large extent, especially at 100 G and higher data rates. Yield is not something unique to just silicon photonics—it is a critical requirement for any practical technology. However, what silicon photonics uniquely enables is particularly important for the industry. First, it allows companies without their own foundry, like Marvell, to design and manufacture highly complex photonic components by leveraging the existing semiconductor infrastructure. Second, it provides true scalability. Marvell, for example, has been one of the well-known companies implementing silicon photonics for applications between datacenters. Intel pioneered silicon photonics for applications inside the datacenters. Today, many of the large transceiver companies have mostly transitioned to silicon photonics. Given this level of adoption, it is fair to say that silicon photonics has already ‘taken over’ in many key segments of the optical interconnect market. Q: Many silicon photonics systems still rely on external laser packaging. Do you see integrated light sources becoming mainstream, or will off-chip lasers dominate for practical reasons? If you look at the integrated laser work published over the years, including our early demonstrations, Intel’s results, and developments at Infinera (now Nokia), it is clear that integrating light sources heterogeneously on silicon or monolithically in InP is commercially viable. The real obstacle is not technical capability, but perception—many people still assume that integrated lasers will suffer from reliability issues. One concern stems from the behavior of indium phosphide lasers at elevated temperatures. It is true that performance can degrade at high temperatures, but when operated around a base temperature of roughly 70–75 °C, these lasers can already operate perfectly well. In fact, Intel has demonstrated strong reliability in this regime. The key is careful design. Temperature variations can be significant, and the resulting wavelength shifts must be properly managed. In practice, engineers have already developed effective strategies to handle these challenges. So, from a technical perspective, I do not see major roadblocks. The bigger challenge may be changing people’s mindset. Let me also make a prediction. Cooling large-scale server infrastructure is becoming increasingly challenging. The industry is already transitioning from air cooling to liquid cooling, and some are exploring immersion cooling. These approaches can provide more stable and potentially lower operating temperatures, which could create an opportunity for integrated lasers as well. Q: How do you see the potential of heterogeneous integration? I think silicon photonics, as a platform for integration, has enormous potential. What we’ve achieved so far is only scratching the surface. There are multiple paths forward here. You can integrate different materials, combine multiple types of devices within the same material platform, or even move toward stacking multiple device layers. All of these approaches are actively being explored, and I believe many of them will enter production in the near future. As data rates continue to increase, heterogeneous integration will also involve incorporating new higher performance materials, opening up more possibilities. At the same time, integration itself is becoming more complex. We’re moving beyond traditional 2D integration. People are now talking about 2.5D, 3D, even 3.5D integration, which will create many new opportunities for system design and performance improvement. Q: You were one of the early voices pushing co-packaged optics (CPO) for AI systems, now more and more companies are doing this. What convinced you CPO was not just a research direction, but something the industry would truly need? The reason I have advocated for CPO stems from my firm belief that heterogeneous integration is the key to high-speed applications. During my time at Inphi (now at Marvell), we were already seeing the limits of traditional approaches. As data rates continued to increase, techniques like wire bonds simply could not keep up. The logical next step was to bring electronics and photonics closer through heterogeneous integration. That was the original motivation—overcoming speed and bandwidth bottlenecks. But as we developed these approaches, it became clear that they also align very well with what CPO is trying to achieve. Over time, the value of co-packaging has expanded beyond just pushing the highest data rates. We now see it being applied even at relatively lower data rates, especially in large-scale AI systems. Whether you look at scale-up, scale-out, or scale-across systems, they all benefit from tightly integrated photonics with electronics. So, in a way, heterogeneous integration is the essence, and CPO is one of its most important applications. Q: Speed is the reason that drives us from ‘pluggable’ to ‘on board’. Many believe ‘pluggable’ can be extended with better DSPs and packaging, while others argue they won’t scale forever’. From your perspective, will this ‘extension strategy’ scale indefinitely? If you had to point to a single limiting factor, what would ultimately block ‘pluggable’ from keeping up with 100 T, or 200 T switch ASICs? In my opinion, with 100 T or 200 T switches and systems at the horizon, you will need co-packaging. It’s true that the industry is very comfortable with ‘pluggable’ solutions, and there is a strong incentive to extend their life as much as possible. In many scale-up systems, copper will continue to be used as long as it makes sense from a power and cost perspective. For the same reasons, data center operators would naturally prefer to stay with pluggable solutions. However, when developing new products, you have to look ahead. Since late last year, analysts have begun including CPO in their revenue projections, which signals a real shift in the industry. In fact, data center operators and hyperscalers are now actively funding CPO developments. That tells you this is no longer just a research direction. Technically, pluggables can be pushed further, even beyond 200 G per lane, but the cost is power. As you scale bandwidth, the power required for high-speed electrical interfaces and DSPs becomes increasingly difficult to manage. This is where CPO becomes very attractive, especially for scale-up architecture like GPU or accelerator interconnects. I actually think the first major deployment for CPO will happen in scale-up systems, and then expand into scale-out. We’re already seeing strong signals from the ecosystem. Nvidia has demonstrated multiple generations of CPO platforms, including at GTC and ISSCC. From a foundry perspective, this aligns very well with TSMC’s roadmap. The idea of tightly coupling electrical compute with optical I/O is no longer a dream—it could be just a year or two away. That said, predicting the exact timing is always difficult. It’s much easier to explain the past than to forecast the future. When you work at the intersection of technology development and product deployment, there is always uncertainty about when the market will be ready. In many ways, developing new technologies can feel like placing a bet—and that’s simply part of innovation. Q: People often say that co-packaging is always the difficult part, what do you think about that? That perception largely comes from how optical packaging used to be done. If you compare it to electrical packaging, the difference is quite clear. Electrical packaging has been highly automated for a long time. But if you look at the optical packaging in early days, you’ll find yourself doing things one at a time, wire bonding electronics and manually aligning fibers, which are slow and not scalable. But once volume kicks in, things start to change. A lot of these processes are now highly automated. There are still operators involved, but the work is largely handled by automatic equipment. Efficient fiber coupling is still a challenge, no question. But there are already solutions out there, and they’re getting better. As volume continues to ramp, I believe the fiber coupling part will gradually be automated as well. If you look at where we are today, photonics manufacturing is already very different from five years ago, and nothing like ten years ago. And honestly, things are only going to move faster from here. Q: Are Through-Silicon Via (TSVs) essential for CPO? We demonstrated and published extensively on TSV as an option way to integrate electronics on top and then route down into silicon photonics bottom layer. But you don’t have to always do that. You can flip the architecture and put the optics on top instead, and some groups are actively exploring that. One reason people hesitate is thermal. Electronics tend to run hotter than optics, so it’s often easier to heat-sink from the top, if the electronics are there. But that’s not a fundamental limitation—there are many alternative thermal management strategies people are exploring as well. There are also practical considerations in the fab that may lead to a preference for one configuration over another. Early on, there was a strong tendency to adopt TSV-based approach. For example, you now have packaging techniques like fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP), which does not require TSVs at all. Several companies are already offering this technology, and some are even beginning to deploy CPO based on it. So, the absence of TSVs is not a fundamental roadblock—there are multiple viable ways to implement these systems. At the end of the day if you want to keep it really simple, all this AI bottleneck this and semi-conductor that, photonics this and space tech that…

All requires mining, hundreds and hundreds of extra mines, processing plants and recycling / refining technology

The oldest and simplest form of investing 

You can buy the shovels or you can buy the materials that make a shovel. Much easier to just buy the materials and care less what niche tech they end up in.

他说的非常对，总结下来就三点：

1. AI 光子学，他们的Photonics部门Q4 2025已实现调整后EBITDA转正至+1600万SEK。 该部已经实现自我造血功能，并不需要公司稀释股份来完成科研。

另外，Sivers与一家未披露的Fortune 100企业建立了长期合作，这是光子业务的核心锚点。 当然也包括我们的老客户 $POET，共同为AI数据中心交付创新光引擎。

2. 5G/6G FR3波束成形

 $sive 在3月份正式发布7-15GHz Daybreak™波束成形IC，面向5G/6G FR3新兴应用及多功能国防阵列。合作方包括雷神（Raytheon）和爱立信（Ericsson）。

3. 激光雷达

该项目的客户将于2026年Q4开始量产，使用Sivers的激光器和放大器。

三辆马车会把 $sive拉得更远……………

YOLO………

text:
the Evercore 1Q26 AI channel checks showing scale-out demand still strong and gated by supply, with scale-up interconnects converging fast toward optical but stuck on protocol and form-factor standardization, is the exact signal that the networking/photonics layer is becoming the next real choke point.

hyperscaler strategies diverging while the physical bottlenecks stay tight is bullish for $MRVL, $AVGO, $MTSI, $ALAB, and $NVDA, but the real edge stays with the names that can actually deliver into the supply-constrained reality.

this is structural, not hype.

hyperscalers are locked into hundreds of billions in committed cluster spend, and the shift to optical interconnects at scale isn’t optional anymore as clusters hit density, heat, and power limits copper can’t solve.

this cycle rewards the execution plays facing proven shortages and shipping product at scale. the AI buildout has years left and the interconnect layer is one of those constraints that can’t be fixed overnight. the setup stays strong.

text:
上个帖子提到的未来将会成为下一个 $LITE的瑞典光子学股票

Sivers Photonics 是 CPO 领域重要的激光器供应商之一，通过供应链映射，它与 $LITE、 $COHR、 $MTSI、Sumitomo、Furukawa 等公司同处这一供应链版图中。

我非常看好 Sivers，因为我发现它映射到了许多超大规模云厂商的供应商链条中。而这些信息，其他人、算法以及更广泛的市场，目前还并不了解。

Sivers（ $SIVE）仍然是 Photonics/CPO 领域中一个并不广为人知、但非常关键的 CW 激光器瓶颈环节。

它映射到 Apple、AMD、Marvell、Amazon、Microsoft、Meta 等公司的客户链条。同时，CPO 是其主要增长向量，并通过 2027 年和 2028 年面向 1.6T/3.2T 的可插拔模块扩展其 TAM。

这份报告包含了一些信息发现，将 Sivers 映射到了许多领先但尚未披露的超大规模云厂商供应商。

公开披露的客户包括：

→ Jabil（NYSE: JBL）：“Sivers Semiconductors 与 Jabil 合作开发高能效 1.6T 可插拔光收发模块”

→ Poet（ $POET）：“POET Technologies 与 Sivers Semiconductors 合作，为共封装光学和下一代 AI 市场开发外部光源”

→ Ayar Labs： $SIVE 作为激光器供应商被列在 Ayar Labs 官网上。

→ O-Net 和 Enablence：“Sivers Semiconductors、O-Net 和 Enablence Technologies 宣布面向 AI 数据中心的外部光源”

→ LIGHTIUM AG（“将其 CW 激光器直接集成到 TFLN 晶圆上”）：2025 年年度报告 PDF。用于 1.6T / 3.2T 的激光器。

高置信度客户包括：

→ Apple（ $AAPL）——用于 Apple 硅光子项目的激光器，尤其是 Apple Watch。

→ Aeva（ $AEVA）——其他分析师报告中经常提到的 LiDAR 客户。

→ Marvell Celestial（ $MRVL）——直接供应商，与 $POET 无关。

可能客户包括：

→ Lightmatter（通过投资映射 + 图像演示资料）

→ Lightelligence（HKG: 1879）——通过投资映射 + 图像演示资料

→ Advanced Micro Devices（ $AMD）——通过 GlobalFoundries 的 CPO 项目公告“AMD Taps GlobalFoundries for MI500’s Co-Packaged Optics”以及激光器供应商映射。

在 CPO/光学供应链之外，Sivers 很可能也是以下客户的供应商：

→ YSS。Sivers 的主要客户 ALLSPACE 被 York 收购，现在已嵌入 YSS 供应链。

→ Golden Dome、Space Force、Space Development Agency 以及 DoD 相关关系，现在都通过 YSS 向上游映射到 Sivers。

其母公司的 CHIPS Act 拨款包括：

→ BAE Systems 等军工承包商，近期获得 660 万美元 CHIPS Act 第二年后续拨款。

→ 与 ERIC 和 Raytheon（RTX）合作的 FR3 波束成形器 IC。

此外，→ NOKIA 也可能是 Sivers 的另一个尚未被市场发现的客户，这一点来自其 2025 年年度报告中的收入/地点映射。

来自 Sivers 主要线索的一跳/两跳客户包括：

Ayar（CPO）：

→ AlChip / GUC / Winwynn，通过 Ayar。主要线索重点是 AlChip 的 CPO。

→ → Amazon 很可能是 Ayar 的主要生态客户：在 Amazon 于 2026 年 5 月进行新的私募投资之后，AlChip 可能赢得了 Trainium 4 设计。AlChip 是 Ayar 的主要客户。

→ → AMD 很有可能成为 Sivers 的主要客户。

可能通过两条路径实现：

Enosemi（AMD 收购后的内部 PIC 设计团队）。Enosemi 的 chiplets 在 GF 代工，但对于 ELS，AMD 可以从多个玩家采购，其中 $SIVE 可能是底层的多来源激光器来源。
Ayar（AMD 于 2026 年 3 月参与其 E 轮投资）。Ayar 的 SuperNova 光源已经使用 $SIVE 的 DFB 激光器阵列。考虑到时间线以及近期巨额融资，Ayar 的 SuperNova 可能是 2027 年 MI500 第一代 CPO 路径。它们都出现在 GFS 关于 AMD / GFS CPO 公告的生态系统幻灯片中，作为生态合作伙伴出现。

→ 其他超大规模云厂商，如 Meta、Microsoft、Google 等，也具有较高可能性。

在 Lumentum 和 Macom 从 Ayar 官网移除之后， $SIVE 很可能成为 Ayar 的主要激光器供应商。

Lumentum 也曾暗示，由于产能过度转向 Nvidia 所需的 EML，其 CW 激光器生产存在问题；根据其财报电话会议记录，他们正在从竞争对手的公开市场采购相关产品。

Sivers 也在其近期发布的年度报告中暗示了与 Win Semi 的产能分配确认，这很可能帮助其赢得 Ayar 的主要供应位置。

text:
the market pricing photonics transition like it’s imminent while the actual builders say copper-to-optics in a rack multiplies cost by x10 is the kind of disconnect that creates real opportunity for the names already shipping market-fit solutions today.

the post is spot on. Eric Cap from Bull/Eviden and the other panelists at the OCP Summit aren’t sell-side analysts; they’re the guys actually building the machines. 

when they say optics is too expensive right now, the pragmatic response is densification, liquid cooling, and specialized compute instead of waiting for photonics to get cheap.

that’s exactly why $AL2SI (2CRSi) and $ALKAL (KALRAY) look well positioned. ultra-dense thermal architecture and DPU MPPA solutions solve the constraints the industry is facing right now without waiting for the next-gen interconnect to drop in price.

i’m biased that this slower photonics ramp is net positive for the European plays that can deliver today. hyperscalers are locked into hundreds of billions in committed cluster spend and they need capacity now, not in 2-3 years when optics economics improve. 

the physical bottlenecks in power, heat, and interconnects don’t pause for the perfect technology; they get solved with what’s available and economical.

this cycle rewards the execution plays that solve proven shortages and ship product at scale right now, not the ones betting on a future cost curve. the setup for $AL2SI and $ALKAL has legs while the broader AI infra buildout keeps rolling.

text:
.@light_con, a developer of femtosecond lasers and laser systems, has acquired Class 5 Photonics, a Hamburg, Germany-based manufacturer of ultrafast laser systems.

Read more here: https://t.co/SlURte3EC9

#Photonics #Lasers

核心总结：Sivers Photonics（$SIVE）是 CPO（共封装光学）领域目前被市场严重低估的核心 CW 激光供应商，它已悄然深度映射到 Apple、AMD、Marvell Celestial、Amazon、Microsoft、Meta 等 hyperscaler 的供应链，并通过 Jabil、POET、Ayar Labs、O-Net、Lightmatter、Lightelligence 等关键伙伴形成广泛覆盖，涵盖可插拔 1.6T/3.2T 模块和共封装光学两大路线。作为激光这一 AI 数据中心光互连的瓶颈环节，SIVE 将直接受益于 2027-2028 年 CPO 超级周期的爆发式增长，具有极高的 TAM 扩张和业绩爆发潜力，目前多数高端客户关系仍未被市场充分认知。

text:
Definitely plausible on a couple contracts and a re-rate for memory/photonics.  5x sales is not a big multiple for a growth company sitting at the intersection of some of the most powerful trends out there right now.  

Oh, and I found that besides the Chair of $LITE also being Chair of $PENG, the CTOs of both $AMD and $SKM sit on the Board.  I like that $SKM has an equity stake too, bc it will deter anyone with ideas of trying to scoop the entire company up on the cheap!

@StockSavvyShay @FuturumEquities Interesting how almost every top performer ties back to AI infrastructure, photonics, memory, or compute. Market keeps rewarding the picks powering the next tech cycle. Smaller stories like $TROO are worth watching early too.

text:
$AEHR / Aehr Test Systems 상세 정리

AEHR은 단순 반도체 장비주가 아니다.

이 회사는 AI 반도체, silicon photonics, SiC/GaN 전력반도체가 복잡해질수록 중요해지는 test & burn-in 병목에 걸려 있다.

AI 반도체가 커질수록 칩은 더 비싸지고, 패키지는 더 복잡해지고, 전력 밀도는 더 올라간다.

고성능 AI ASIC, GPU, optical I/O, silicon photonics, SiC/GaN power devices는 모두 높은 신뢰성이 필요하다.

문제 있는 칩을 패키징 후반이나 고객 단계에서 발견하면 손실이 매우 크다.

그래서 wafer-level burn-in, package-level burn-in, reliability test가 중요해진다.

AEHR은 바로 이 지점에 있다.

정확히 말하면 AEHR은 AI processor ASIC package-level burn-in, silicon photonics wafer-level burn-in, SiC/GaN power semiconductor burn-in 장비 회사다.

핵심 제품군은 FOX-XP, FOX-NP, WaferPak, Sonoma 같은 test & burn-in 플랫폼이다.

이 회사가 흥미로운 이유는 단순 테마가 아니라 고객 주문이 실제로 찍혔기 때문이다.

2026년 4월 AEHR은 lead hyperscale AI customer로부터 4,100만 달러 규모의 사상 최대 follow-on production order를 받았다고 발표했다.

이 주문은 데이터센터 training 및 inference AI workload에 쓰이는 custom AI processor ASIC의 high-volume package-level burn-in을 지원하는 주문이다.

주문에는 Sonoma high-power package-level test and burn-in systems, burn-in modules, device-specific sockets가 포함된다.

이건 꽤 중요하다.

대부분의 병목 리포트에서 가장 약한 부분은 “고객 증거”다.

그런데 AEHR은 최소한 특정 lead hyperscale AI customer가 있고, 이 고객이 이미 생산용 주문을 냈다.

고객명은 공개되지 않았지만, 용도·주문 규모·제품·납품 시점이 비교적 명확하다.

AEHR은 이 주문 하나로 끝나는 것도 아니다.

회사는 이 4,100만 달러 주문을 포함해 fiscal second half bookings가 이미 9,200만 달러 이상이라고 밝혔다.

즉 현재 매출보다 앞으로 들어올 주문 가시성이 훨씬 중요해진 상황이다.

AEHR의 첫 번째 핵심 축은 custom AI processor ASIC package-level burn-in이다.

이건 SIVE나 AXTI와 다르다.

SIVE는 AI optical link용 DFB laser와 photonics 협력 근거가 있다. 하지만 아직 대량 양산 매출이 충분히 확인되지는 않았다.

AXTI는 InP substrate 병목 논지가 강하다. 하지만 고객별 물량과 최종 플랫폼 노출은 더 확인해야 한다.

반면 AEHR은 적어도 AI processor ASIC burn-in 쪽에서는 대형 고객의 production order가 이미 찍힌 상태다.

이 점 때문에 AEHR은 새 후보 중에서 꽤 눈에 띈다.

AEHR의 두 번째 축은 silicon photonics wafer-level burn-in이다.

2026년 3월 AEHR은 silicon photonics 분야의 major new customer로부터 주문을 받았다고 발표했다.

해당 고객은 글로벌 networking products and solutions 리더이자, data center optical transceiver 시장의 주요 공급자라고 설명됐다.

이 고객은 hyperscale AI와 cloud data center의 high-speed fiber optic communication link 수요를 겨냥해 silicon photonics 기반 transceiver를 개발 중이다.

이 주문에는 engineering qualification과 high-volume production을 위한 시스템들이 포함됐다.

이건 CPO/광학 병목과 직접 연결된다.

AI 데이터센터가 copper에서 fiber optic interconnect로 이동하면 silicon photonics transceiver의 신뢰성 검증이 중요해진다.

AEHR은 이 silicon photonics 제조 흐름에서 wafer-level burn-in이 점점 중요해질 수 있다고 보고 있다.

즉 AEHR은 AI ASIC burn-in + silicon photonics burn-in을 동시에 갖고 있다.

이 조합이 좋다.

지금 AI 병목 흐름은 CPO/광학, AI 데이터센터 전력, AI ASIC, silicon photonics 쪽으로 퍼지고 있다.

AEHR은 이 중 두 개 이상에 직접 걸린다.

광학이 커지면 silicon photonics WLBI 수요가 생긴다.

AI ASIC이 커지면 high-power package-level burn-in 수요가 생긴다.

SiC/GaN power semiconductor가 커지면 wafer-level burn-in 수요가 생긴다.

즉 AEHR은 단일 테마가 아니라 여러 병목 레이어의 test infrastructure에 걸린 회사다.

하지만 현재 실적만 보면 아직 작다.

FY2026 Q3 매출은 약 1,030만 달러였다.

전년 동기보다 낮다.

GAAP 순손실도 있었고, Non-GAAP 기준으로도 손실이 남아 있다.

즉 AEHR은 아직 “실적이 이미 폭발한 회사”가 아니다.

오히려 현재 매출은 작고, 주가는 미래 주문과 FY2027 매출 전환을 선반영하고 있다.

이게 핵심이다.

AEHR은 현재 손익보다 bookings와 FY2027 매출 전환을 봐야 하는 종목이다.

최근 bookings는 강하다.

FY2026 Q3 bookings는 약 3,720만 달러였고, 이후 4,100만 달러 hyperscale AI customer production order가 추가되면서 fiscal second half bookings가 9,200만 달러를 넘었다.

이 구조가 AEHR의 핵심이다.

현재 매출은 작지만, 주문이 앞에서 쌓이고 있다.

그래서 이 회사는 “현재 실적주”가 아니라 FY2027 이후 주문이 매출로 바뀌는지를 보는 고베타 병목주다.

현재 밸류에이션은 높다.

최근 기준 AEHR 주가는 약 99달러대, 시가총액은 약 30억 달러 수준이다.

회사의 FY2026 매출은 대략 4,500만~5,000만 달러 범위로 예상된다.

즉 현재 매출 대비 시총은 매우 높다.

이건 싸다고 볼 수 없다.

AEHR은 저평가주가 아니다.

정확히는 FY2027 이후 AI ASIC / silicon photonics / SiC-GaN burn-in 수요가 매출로 크게 전환된다는 기대를 사는 고밸류 고베타 병목주다.

그래도 볼 만한 이유는 명확하다.

첫째, 고객 주문의 질이 좋다.

4,100만 달러 주문은 단순 평가용 주문이 아니라 high-volume production burn-in용 주문이다.

대상도 custom AI processor ASIC이고, 고객은 lead hyperscale customer다.

이건 병목주에서 매우 강한 근거다.

둘째, consumables 모델이 있다.

주문에는 burn-in modules와 device-specific sockets가 포함된다.

이런 부품은 시스템 설치 이후에도 고객 ramp, 차세대 ASIC 전환, 제품별 세팅에 따라 반복 매출이 생길 수 있다.

장비를 팔고 끝나는 구조가 아닐 가능성이 있다.

셋째, silicon photonics와도 연결된다.

AI 데이터센터 optical interconnect가 커질수록 silicon photonics transceiver 품질 검증이 중요해진다.

AEHR은 이미 major networking / optical transceiver customer를 확보했고, qualification과 high-volume production용 시스템을 주문받았다.

넷째, SiC/GaN power semiconductor test까지 옵션이 있다.

AI processor, silicon photonics, silicon carbide, gallium nitride power semiconductor wafer-level production burn-in이 모두 잠재 시장이다.

즉 AEHR은 AI compute, AI optical, AI power semiconductor 세 방향에 걸친다.

다섯째, 생산능력 확장도 진행 중이다.

회사는 Fremont 시설에 power, cooling, clean-room manufacturing space를 추가했고, Sonoma 시스템 생산을 위한 contract manufacturing capacity도 준비 중이다.

주문이 들어왔을 때 생산이 가능해야 하는데, 이 부분을 준비하고 있다는 점은 긍정적이다.

하지만 리스크도 크다.

첫째, 현재 매출 대비 시총이 매우 높다.

FY2026 매출 5,000만 달러 안팎 회사가 시총 30억 달러 이상에서 거래된다.

즉 시장은 FY2027~2028 매출 램프를 강하게 선반영하고 있다.

둘째, 고객 집중 리스크가 있다.

Lead hyperscale customer 하나의 주문이 매우 중요하다.

이 고객의 ramp가 늦어지거나, 차세대 ASIC 일정이 지연되거나, 테스트 방식이 바뀌면 AEHR 매출이 크게 흔들릴 수 있다.

셋째, 주문은 매출이 아니다.

Bookings가 좋다고 바로 매출과 이익이 되는 것은 아니다.

시스템 납품, 고객 acceptance, revenue recognition, production ramp가 필요하다.

넷째, silicon photonics도 아직 초기 ramp다.

Major customer win은 좋지만, 전체 silicon photonics WLBI 시장이 얼마나 커질지는 확인이 필요하다.

다섯째, 아직 적자 회사다.

매출이 늘면 operating leverage가 생길 수 있지만, 지금은 아직 손실이 남아 있다.

여섯째, 주가가 이미 많이 올랐다.

현재 가격은 좋은 뉴스 상당 부분을 반영한다.

추가 주문이 계속 나오지 않으면 주가가 쉬어갈 수 있다.

시나리오는 이렇게 본다.

Base Case.

AEHR이 FY2027에 4,100만 달러 AI ASIC 주문과 silicon photonics 주문을 매출로 전환하지만, 추가 고객 확장이 생각보다 느린 경우다.

이 경우 현재 시총은 이미 기대를 많이 반영했기 때문에 현재가 대비 0.7~1.2배 정도가 현실적이다.

주가로는 대략 70~120달러 구간이다.

Base Case에서는 좋은 회사지만 비싼 주식이다.

Bull Case.

Lead hyperscale customer의 production order가 반복되고, 차세대 AI accelerator ASIC용 Sonoma 수요가 더 커지며, silicon photonics customer도 follow-on order를 내는 경우다.

이 경우 FY2027~2028 매출이 현재 5,000만 달러 수준에서 1.5억~2.5억 달러 이상으로 올라갈 수 있다.

이 경우 현재가 대비 2~4배를 볼 수 있다.

주가로는 대략 200~400달러 구간이다.

이 시나리오에서는 AEHR이 단순 test equipment company가 아니라 AI ASIC과 silicon photonics burn-in 병목주로 재평가된다.

Extreme Bull Case.

AI hyperscaler들이 custom ASIC을 계속 확대하고, 고전력 AI processor burn-in이 필수 공정으로 자리 잡으며, silicon photonics transceiver WLBI와 SiC/GaN power semiconductor burn-in까지 동시에 ramp되는 경우다.

이 경우 AEHR은 AI compute, AI optical, AI power semiconductor 세 시장에 모두 걸친 reliability test infrastructure company로 재평가될 수 있다.

이 경우 현재가 대비 5~8배까지도 가능하다.

주가로는 대략 500~800달러 구간이다.

다만 이건 매우 공격적인 상단 시나리오다.

이 시나리오가 나오려면 조건이 필요하다.

Lead hyperscale customer 주문이 반복되어야 한다.

AI ASIC 고객이 2개 이상으로 늘어나야 한다.

Silicon photonics 고객이 high-volume production으로 이어져야 한다.

SiC/GaN burn-in 수요도 실제 매출로 붙어야 한다.

Gross margin과 operating margin이 장비주답게 올라와야 한다.

Consumables 매출이 반복적으로 쌓여야 한다.

AEHR을 SIVE/PENG/WOLF와 비교하면 꽤 흥미롭다.

SIVE는 photonics 제품 자체에 대한 고베타다.

상단은 크지만 아직 양산 매출과 ASP/물량 증거가 약하다.

PENG는 실적이 있는 AI infrastructure / Integrated Memory 회사다.

안정성은 좋지만 상단은 상대적으로 낮다.

WOLF는 SiC 전력반도체 턴어라운드다.

상단은 크지만 gross margin이 음수다.

AEHR은 그 중간 어딘가다.

SIVE보다 고객 주문이 더 직접적이다.

PENG보다 고베타다.

WOLF보다 현재 사업 구조가 덜 망가져 있다.

다만 AEHR도 이미 밸류에이션이 높고, 고객 집중 리스크가 있다.

그래서 내 기준 AEHR은 포트의 코어보다는 10~15% 고베타 병목 추가 후보에 가깝다.

AEHR의 핵심 체크포인트는 다음과 같다.

4,100만 달러 AI ASIC production order가 FY2027 매출로 계획대로 인식되는가.

Lead hyperscale customer의 차세대 AI accelerator ASIC용 추가 주문이 나오는가.

Silicon photonics 고객이 qualification에서 high-volume production으로 넘어가는가.

Follow-on orders가 실제로 나오는가.

Sonoma 시스템 생산능력 확대가 매출 전환을 뒷받침하는가.

Consumables, sockets, BIMs 매출이 반복적으로 쌓이는가.

SiC/GaN power semiconductor burn-in 수요가 실제 고객 주문으로 이어지는가.

Gross margin과 operating margin이 매출 증가와 함께 올라오는가.

고객이 한두 곳에 과도하게 집중되지 않는가.

현재 높은 valuation을 정당화할 만큼 FY2027 가이던스가 크게 올라가는가.

Kill Conditions도 분명하다.

Lead hyperscale customer 추가 주문이 지연되면 Bull Case는 약해진다.

4,100만 달러 주문의 납품 또는 매출 인식이 밀리면 주가는 크게 흔들릴 수 있다.

Silicon photonics 고객이 high-volume production으로 넘어가지 못하면 광학 병목 논지는 약해진다.

FY2027 매출 가이던스가 시장 기대보다 낮으면 valuation 부담이 커진다.

Gross margin이 기대보다 낮게 나오면 장비주 프리미엄은 낮아진다.

고객 집중도가 높고 신규 고객 확장이 느리면 멀티플 확장은 제한된다.

SiC/GaN power semiconductor burn-in 수요가 실제 주문으로 연결되지 않으면 시장 확장 논지는 축소된다.

현재 시총이 이미 높기 때문에 작은 실망에도 조정이 클 수 있다.

최종 결론은 이렇다.

AEHR은 매우 흥미로운 병목주다.

이 회사는 AI ASIC, silicon photonics, SiC/GaN power semiconductor라는 세 개의 고성장 병목 레이어에 걸린다.

특히 4,100만 달러 hyperscale AI customer production order와 silicon photonics customer win은 단순 테마가 아니라 실제 고객 주문이라는 점에서 강하다.

이 점은 SIVE보다 더 직접적인 근거다.

다만 현재 매출은 아직 작고, 시총은 이미 30억 달러를 넘었다.

따라서 AEHR은 저평가주가 아니다.

정확히는 FY2027 이후 AI ASIC / silicon photonics burn-in 매출이 폭발적으로 늘어날 경우 재평가될 수 있는 고밸류 고베타 병목주다.

AI 반도체가 커질수록 칩은 더 비싸지고, 패키지는 더 복잡해지고, 전력 밀도는 더 올라간다.", "id_str": "2055734918928572566", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "ko", "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 4, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "1614496714030993414"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [{"indices": [0, 5], "text": "AEHR"}], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNTU3MzQ5MTg0ODgyMjc4NDA=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "$AEHR / Aehr Test Systems 상세 정리 AEHR은 단순 반도체 장비주가 아니다. 이 회사는 AI 반도체, silicon photonics, SiC/GaN 전력반도체가 복잡해질수록 중요해지는 test & burn-in 병목에 걸려 있다. AI 반도체가 커질수록 칩은 더 비싸지고, 패키지는 더 복잡해지고, 전력 밀도는 더 올라간다. 고성능 AI ASIC, GPU, optical I/O, silicon photonics, SiC/GaN power devices는 모두 높은 신뢰성이 필요하다. 문제 있는 칩을 패키징 후반이나 고객 단계에서 발견하면 손실이 매우 크다. 그래서 wafer-level burn-in, package-level burn-in, reliability test가 중요해진다. AEHR은 바로 이 지점에 있다. 정확히 말하면 AEHR은 AI processor ASIC package-level burn-in, silicon photonics wafer-level burn-in, SiC/GaN power semiconductor burn-in 장비 회사다. 핵심 제품군은 FOX-XP, FOX-NP, WaferPak, Sonoma 같은 test & burn-in 플랫폼이다. 이 회사가 흥미로운 이유는 단순 테마가 아니라 고객 주문이 실제로 찍혔기 때문이다. 2026년 4월 AEHR은 lead hyperscale AI customer로부터 4,100만 달러 규모의 사상 최대 follow-on production order를 받았다고 발표했다. 이 주문은 데이터센터 training 및 inference AI workload에 쓰이는 custom AI processor ASIC의 high-volume package-level burn-in을 지원하는 주문이다. 주문에는 Sonoma high-power package-level test and burn-in systems, burn-in modules, device-specific sockets가 포함된다. 이건 꽤 중요하다. 대부분의 병목 리포트에서 가장 약한 부분은 “고객 증거”다. 그런데 AEHR은 최소한 특정 lead hyperscale AI customer가 있고, 이 고객이 이미 생산용 주문을 냈다. 고객명은 공개되지 않았지만, 용도·주문 규모·제품·납품 시점이 비교적 명확하다. AEHR은 이 주문 하나로 끝나는 것도 아니다. 회사는 이 4,100만 달러 주문을 포함해 fiscal second half bookings가 이미 9,200만 달러 이상이라고 밝혔다. 즉 현재 매출보다 앞으로 들어올 주문 가시성이 훨씬 중요해진 상황이다. AEHR의 첫 번째 핵심 축은 custom AI processor ASIC package-level burn-in이다. 이건 SIVE나 AXTI와 다르다. SIVE는 AI optical link용 DFB laser와 photonics 협력 근거가 있다. 하지만 아직 대량 양산 매출이 충분히 확인되지는 않았다. AXTI는 InP substrate 병목 논지가 강하다. 하지만 고객별 물량과 최종 플랫폼 노출은 더 확인해야 한다. 반면 AEHR은 적어도 AI processor ASIC burn-in 쪽에서는 대형 고객의 production order가 이미 찍힌 상태다. 이 점 때문에 AEHR은 새 후보 중에서 꽤 눈에 띈다. AEHR의 두 번째 축은 silicon photonics wafer-level burn-in이다. 2026년 3월 AEHR은 silicon photonics 분야의 major new customer로부터 주문을 받았다고 발표했다. 해당 고객은 글로벌 networking products and solutions 리더이자, data center optical transceiver 시장의 주요 공급자라고 설명됐다. 이 고객은 hyperscale AI와 cloud data center의 high-speed fiber optic communication link 수요를 겨냥해 silicon photonics 기반 transceiver를 개발 중이다. 이 주문에는 engineering qualification과 high-volume production을 위한 시스템들이 포함됐다. 이건 CPO/광학 병목과 직접 연결된다. AI 데이터센터가 copper에서 fiber optic interconnect로 이동하면 silicon photonics transceiver의 신뢰성 검증이 중요해진다. AEHR은 이 silicon photonics 제조 흐름에서 wafer-level burn-in이 점점 중요해질 수 있다고 보고 있다. 즉 AEHR은 AI ASIC burn-in + silicon photonics burn-in을 동시에 갖고 있다. 이 조합이 좋다. 지금 AI 병목 흐름은 CPO/광학, AI 데이터센터 전력, AI ASIC, silicon photonics 쪽으로 퍼지고 있다. AEHR은 이 중 두 개 이상에 직접 걸린다. 광학이 커지면 silicon photonics WLBI 수요가 생긴다. AI ASIC이 커지면 high-power package-level burn-in 수요가 생긴다. SiC/GaN power semiconductor가 커지면 wafer-level burn-in 수요가 생긴다. 즉 AEHR은 단일 테마가 아니라 여러 병목 레이어의 test infrastructure에 걸린 회사다. 하지만 현재 실적만 보면 아직 작다. FY2026 Q3 매출은 약 1,030만 달러였다. 전년 동기보다 낮다. GAAP 순손실도 있었고, Non-GAAP 기준으로도 손실이 남아 있다. 즉 AEHR은 아직 “실적이 이미 폭발한 회사”가 아니다. 오히려 현재 매출은 작고, 주가는 미래 주문과 FY2027 매출 전환을 선반영하고 있다. 이게 핵심이다. AEHR은 현재 손익보다 bookings와 FY2027 매출 전환을 봐야 하는 종목이다. 최근 bookings는 강하다. FY2026 Q3 bookings는 약 3,720만 달러였고, 이후 4,100만 달러 hyperscale AI customer production order가 추가되면서 fiscal second half bookings가 9,200만 달러를 넘었다. 이 구조가 AEHR의 핵심이다. 현재 매출은 작지만, 주문이 앞에서 쌓이고 있다. 그래서 이 회사는 “현재 실적주”가 아니라 FY2027 이후 주문이 매출로 바뀌는지를 보는 고베타 병목주다. 현재 밸류에이션은 높다. 최근 기준 AEHR 주가는 약 99달러대, 시가총액은 약 30억 달러 수준이다. 회사의 FY2026 매출은 대략 4,500만~5,000만 달러 범위로 예상된다. 즉 현재 매출 대비 시총은 매우 높다. 이건 싸다고 볼 수 없다. AEHR은 저평가주가 아니다. 정확히는 FY2027 이후 AI ASIC / silicon photonics / SiC-GaN burn-in 수요가 매출로 크게 전환된다는 기대를 사는 고밸류 고베타 병목주다. 그래도 볼 만한 이유는 명확하다. 첫째, 고객 주문의 질이 좋다. 4,100만 달러 주문은 단순 평가용 주문이 아니라 high-volume production burn-in용 주문이다. 대상도 custom AI processor ASIC이고, 고객은 lead hyperscale customer다. 이건 병목주에서 매우 강한 근거다. 둘째, consumables 모델이 있다. 주문에는 burn-in modules와 device-specific sockets가 포함된다. 이런 부품은 시스템 설치 이후에도 고객 ramp, 차세대 ASIC 전환, 제품별 세팅에 따라 반복 매출이 생길 수 있다. 장비를 팔고 끝나는 구조가 아닐 가능성이 있다. 셋째, silicon photonics와도 연결된다. AI 데이터센터 optical interconnect가 커질수록 silicon photonics transceiver 품질 검증이 중요해진다. AEHR은 이미 major networking / optical transceiver customer를 확보했고, qualification과 high-volume production용 시스템을 주문받았다. 넷째, SiC/GaN power semiconductor test까지 옵션이 있다. AI processor, silicon photonics, silicon carbide, gallium nitride power semiconductor wafer-level production burn-in이 모두 잠재 시장이다. 즉 AEHR은 AI compute, AI optical, AI power semiconductor 세 방향에 걸친다. 다섯째, 생산능력 확장도 진행 중이다. 회사는 Fremont 시설에 power, cooling, clean-room manufacturing space를 추가했고, Sonoma 시스템 생산을 위한 contract manufacturing capacity도 준비 중이다. 주문이 들어왔을 때 생산이 가능해야 하는데, 이 부분을 준비하고 있다는 점은 긍정적이다. 하지만 리스크도 크다. 첫째, 현재 매출 대비 시총이 매우 높다. FY2026 매출 5,000만 달러 안팎 회사가 시총 30억 달러 이상에서 거래된다. 즉 시장은 FY2027~2028 매출 램프를 강하게 선반영하고 있다. 둘째, 고객 집중 리스크가 있다. Lead hyperscale customer 하나의 주문이 매우 중요하다. 이 고객의 ramp가 늦어지거나, 차세대 ASIC 일정이 지연되거나, 테스트 방식이 바뀌면 AEHR 매출이 크게 흔들릴 수 있다. 셋째, 주문은 매출이 아니다. Bookings가 좋다고 바로 매출과 이익이 되는 것은 아니다. 시스템 납품, 고객 acceptance, revenue recognition, production ramp가 필요하다. 넷째, silicon photonics도 아직 초기 ramp다. Major customer win은 좋지만, 전체 silicon photonics WLBI 시장이 얼마나 커질지는 확인이 필요하다. 다섯째, 아직 적자 회사다. 매출이 늘면 operating leverage가 생길 수 있지만, 지금은 아직 손실이 남아 있다. 여섯째, 주가가 이미 많이 올랐다. 현재 가격은 좋은 뉴스 상당 부분을 반영한다. 추가 주문이 계속 나오지 않으면 주가가 쉬어갈 수 있다. 시나리오는 이렇게 본다. Base Case. AEHR이 FY2027에 4,100만 달러 AI ASIC 주문과 silicon photonics 주문을 매출로 전환하지만, 추가 고객 확장이 생각보다 느린 경우다. 이 경우 현재 시총은 이미 기대를 많이 반영했기 때문에 현재가 대비 0.7~1.2배 정도가 현실적이다. 주가로는 대략 70~120달러 구간이다. Base Case에서는 좋은 회사지만 비싼 주식이다. Bull Case. Lead hyperscale customer의 production order가 반복되고, 차세대 AI accelerator ASIC용 Sonoma 수요가 더 커지며, silicon photonics customer도 follow-on order를 내는 경우다. 이 경우 FY2027~2028 매출이 현재 5,000만 달러 수준에서 1.5억~2.5억 달러 이상으로 올라갈 수 있다. 이 경우 현재가 대비 2~4배를 볼 수 있다. 주가로는 대략 200~400달러 구간이다. 이 시나리오에서는 AEHR이 단순 test equipment company가 아니라 AI ASIC과 silicon photonics burn-in 병목주로 재평가된다. Extreme Bull Case. AI hyperscaler들이 custom ASIC을 계속 확대하고, 고전력 AI processor burn-in이 필수 공정으로 자리 잡으며, silicon photonics transceiver WLBI와 SiC/GaN power semiconductor burn-in까지 동시에 ramp되는 경우다. 이 경우 AEHR은 AI compute, AI optical, AI power semiconductor 세 시장에 모두 걸친 reliability test infrastructure company로 재평가될 수 있다. 이 경우 현재가 대비 5~8배까지도 가능하다. 주가로는 대략 500~800달러 구간이다. 다만 이건 매우 공격적인 상단 시나리오다. 이 시나리오가 나오려면 조건이 필요하다. Lead hyperscale customer 주문이 반복되어야 한다. AI ASIC 고객이 2개 이상으로 늘어나야 한다. Silicon photonics 고객이 high-volume production으로 이어져야 한다. SiC/GaN burn-in 수요도 실제 매출로 붙어야 한다. Gross margin과 operating margin이 장비주답게 올라와야 한다. Consumables 매출이 반복적으로 쌓여야 한다. AEHR을 SIVE/PENG/WOLF와 비교하면 꽤 흥미롭다. SIVE는 photonics 제품 자체에 대한 고베타다. 상단은 크지만 아직 양산 매출과 ASP/물량 증거가 약하다. PENG는 실적이 있는 AI infrastructure / Integrated Memory 회사다. 안정성은 좋지만 상단은 상대적으로 낮다. WOLF는 SiC 전력반도체 턴어라운드다. 상단은 크지만 gross margin이 음수다. AEHR은 그 중간 어딘가다. SIVE보다 고객 주문이 더 직접적이다. PENG보다 고베타다. WOLF보다 현재 사업 구조가 덜 망가져 있다. 다만 AEHR도 이미 밸류에이션이 높고, 고객 집중 리스크가 있다. 그래서 내 기준 AEHR은 포트의 코어보다는 10~15% 고베타 병목 추가 후보에 가깝다. AEHR의 핵심 체크포인트는 다음과 같다. 4,100만 달러 AI ASIC production order가 FY2027 매출로 계획대로 인식되는가. Lead hyperscale customer의 차세대 AI accelerator ASIC용 추가 주문이 나오는가. Silicon photonics 고객이 qualification에서 high-volume production으로 넘어가는가. Follow-on orders가 실제로 나오는가. Sonoma 시스템 생산능력 확대가 매출 전환을 뒷받침하는가. Consumables, sockets, BIMs 매출이 반복적으로 쌓이는가. SiC/GaN power semiconductor burn-in 수요가 실제 고객 주문으로 이어지는가. Gross margin과 operating margin이 매출 증가와 함께 올라오는가. 고객이 한두 곳에 과도하게 집중되지 않는가. 현재 높은 valuation을 정당화할 만큼 FY2027 가이던스가 크게 올라가는가. Kill Conditions도 분명하다. Lead hyperscale customer 추가 주문이 지연되면 Bull Case는 약해진다. 4,100만 달러 주문의 납품 또는 매출 인식이 밀리면 주가는 크게 흔들릴 수 있다. Silicon photonics 고객이 high-volume production으로 넘어가지 못하면 광학 병목 논지는 약해진다. FY2027 매출 가이던스가 시장 기대보다 낮으면 valuation 부담이 커진다. Gross margin이 기대보다 낮게 나오면 장비주 프리미엄은 낮아진다. 고객 집중도가 높고 신규 고객 확장이 느리면 멀티플 확장은 제한된다. SiC/GaN power semiconductor burn-in 수요가 실제 주문으로 연결되지 않으면 시장 확장 논지는 축소된다. 현재 시총이 이미 높기 때문에 작은 실망에도 조정이 클 수 있다. 최종 결론은 이렇다. AEHR은 매우 흥미로운 병목주다. 이 회사는 AI ASIC, silicon photonics, SiC/GaN power semiconductor라는 세 개의 고성장 병목 레이어에 걸린다. 특히 4,100만 달러 hyperscale AI customer production order와 silicon photonics customer win은 단순 테마가 아니라 실제 고객 주문이라는 점에서 강하다. 이 점은 SIVE보다 더 직접적인 근거다. 다만 현재 매출은 아직 작고, 시총은 이미 30억 달러를 넘었다. 따라서 AEHR은 저평가주가 아니다. 정확히는 FY2027 이후 AI ASIC / silicon photonics burn-in 매출이 폭발적으로 늘어날 경우 재평가될 수 있는 고밸류 고베타 병목주다."}}}, "rest_id": "2055734918928572566", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1878", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Meet the Cutting Edge Optronics team at CLEO 2026, May 19–20 in Charlotte, NC at Booth #519.

Scott Taylor, Wolfgang Schlichting, and Faming Xu, PhD will be onsite to discuss DPSS lasers, laser diode arrays, and photonics technologies.

#CLEO2026 #Photonics #Lasers https://t.co/L78WT2GJq2

Coherent is less prominent than NVDA or ASML. That's part of why the opportunity is interesting.

The photonics transition in AI infrastructure is real and the market hasn't caught up to it.

COHR at 11% is the biggest underappreciated chokepoint in this portfolio. #COHR #Photonics

text:
🚨 OPTICAL NETWORKING / AI PHOTONICS STOCKS ARE GETTING HIT HARD TODAY 👀

Major weakness across the AI optics trade:

🔻 $AAOI Applied Optoelectronics: -9.46%
🔻 $LITE Lumentum: -9.13%
🔻 $COHR Coherent: -5.20%
🧠 WHAT’S HAPPENING?
This looks like:

⚡ AI infrastructure de-risking
⚡ semiconductor selloff spillover
⚡ profit taking in high-beta AI names
⚡ valuation compression

These stocks had become HUGE beneficiaries of:
the AI data center boom.

⚡ WHY OPTICS MATTER FOR AI

AI data centers require MASSIVE amounts of:
🟢 optical interconnects
🟢 lasers
🟢 transceivers
🟢 photonics networking
Why?
Because GPUs need:
⚡ ultra-fast communication
between:
servers
clusters
memory
and networking systems.

Without optics:
AI scaling breaks.
🔻 $AAOI + $LITE HIT HARDEST

These names are highly sensitive to:

⚡ hyperscaler capex expectations
⚡ Nvidia ecosystem sentiment
⚡ AI networking demand

The market may currently fear:
expectations ran TOO hot too quickly.

🧠 IMPORTANT CONTEXT
Long term:
the AI optical demand story remains extremely powerful.
AI clusters are becoming:
⚡ larger
⚡ denser
⚡ more bandwidth-intensive
That structurally benefits:
🟢 optics
🟢 photonics
🟢 networking infrastructure

🔥 WHY THIS SECTOR IS SO VOLATILE

Optical/networking stocks tend to be:
🔻 high beta
🔻 cyclical
🔻 sentiment-driven
🔻 hyperscaler-spending dependent
So when AI sentiment weakens:
they often move MORE aggressively than software names.
📈 BIGGER AI MARKET SHIFT
The market is increasingly rotating from:
⚡ “infinite AI capex optimism”
toward:
⚡ execution
⚡ margins
⚡ monetization
⚡ demand sustainability

That shift is hitting:
AI hardware hardest.
⚠️ BUT WATCH THIS CAREFULLY

If Nvidia earnings remain strong…
or hyperscaler AI spending accelerates further…

These optics names could rebound VERY aggressively.

This remains one of the most important AI infrastructure sub-sectors.

💭 CONCLUSION
Today’s selloff reinforces how fragile positioning has become across AI hardware trades.
But structurally:
AI still requires:
⚡ enormous networking bandwidth
⚡ optical scaling
⚡ photonics infrastructure

The long-term AI networking thesis remains very much alive —
even if volatility is getting brutal.

text:
COMPUTING IS BECOMING THE “NEW OIL” 

The first AI trade was hardware: #chips, #memory, #photonics.

But none of that matters without live compute capacity.

That is why #NeoClouds matter.

I see the sector in 4 layers:

Tier 1: full-stack leaders  
Power + data centers + GPUs + software + enterprise customers.  

Best quality, but already crowded and priced. $NBIS is the cleanest winner.

Tier 2: power-secured AI infra operators  
Real power + data centers + GPUs + growing platform/software layer.  

Less crowded, still catching up, already monetizing. 

This is where I place $DGXX.

Tier 3: pure GPU rental  
Good cycle trade, but less structural.

Tier 4: power / land optionality  
Big upside, but still earlier in conversion.

To me, Tier 2 is the best alpha zone:

- not fully priced like Tier 1,  
- more real than Tier 3,  
- further along than Tier 4.

That is why $DGXX still looks under-discovered to me. 

Added more during dip today, will HOLD for long term investment. 

Risk/reward details are in my previous tweet.

[62, 67], "text": "LITE"}], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNTY0MDUzNjk1NjQ0MTgwNDg=", "text": "Share price is getting smoked, but today's fireside chat with $LITE's CEO contained many bullish developments that have emerged since OFC: 1) Transceiver biz is accelerating and expects sales to double over the next 4-5 quarters 2) Scale across opportunity has notably expanded 3) Demand outlook for both scale out and scale up CPO has continued to expand 4) The Co is looking to add yet another fab given how strong demand signals are 5) Noted how difficult it is to run 6 inch wafers and how competitors have been struggling with it, which seems to indicate that Coherent is still having issues with ramping up its 6 inch process. 6) Are in active discussions with several customers about LT supply agreements in exchange for prepayments which I presume would be used to finance their next fab. 7) OCS opportunity has further expanded since OFC. 8) Continue to expect ELS to be a substantial portion of their CPO sales (ELS carries 2x the ASP of UHP lasers) & believe it is a matter of when not if they get their first ELS purchase sometime over the coming months. 9) Are very confident in the durability of their moat, especially OCS and UHP lasers, given that these technologies were developed and proven out over a 10-15 yr timeframe. Overall, this fireside chat reinforced why I continue to believe LITE remains the best risk adjusted investment in the optics space."}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2056405369732165647", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11567", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2056768067045044544", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "398", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
AI photonics continues gaining momentum as data centers search for faster and more efficient optical interconnect technologies.

POET Technologies has secured $400 million in financing to help expand manufacturing infrastructure, scale R&D, accelerate its light source business, and support growing demand from AI and data center markets. The company is focused on photonic integrated circuits, optical engines, and optical interconnect technologies designed for next-generation AI infrastructure. 

The funding comes as POET and Lumilens also advance wafer-level photonic integration technologies aimed at improving bandwidth density, power efficiency, and scalability for future AI optical networks. 

A fascinating look at how photonics manufacturing and optical interconnect technologies are scaling alongside AI infrastructure growth.

Read the full article here:

text:
Pour $SOI le marché price déjà le scénario photonics 2028 de Morgan Stanley dans le cours actuel. Tu paies aujourd'hui environ ~25x EV/EBITDA pour une boîte en perte nette au H1. le marché price déjà le meilleur scénario, donc le risk/reward est symétrique.  

Si l'adoption de la photonique est rapide brutale, et que le bottleneck se matérialise elle grimpera encore. 

Mais si au contraire il y'a le moindre retard, que les industriels sont sceptiques parce que la photonique est trop chère et privilégies d'autres alternatives (comme l'ultra densification par ex) $SOI est déjà trop chère.

Kalray c'est l'IP propriétaire (MPPA) sur un marché DPU en pleine structuration. avec un modèle qui passe au licensing/co-développement (léger en capital), et un catalyseur binaire imminent (OpenChip peut prendre 51% d'ici juillet 2026). à €127M de cap, le marché ne price pas grand chose et reste toujours prduent. 
Mais ça reste un pari également, juste moins fortement valorisé à ce stade.

text:
$SCIA looks interesting again after a breakout quarter. Trading at single digit run-rate EPS w record backlog, no debt and 99%+ of revs tied to the photonics industry.

SCIA's sputtering targets (mainly ITO) show up in dozens of peer-reviewed studies next to Coherent's excimer lasers. Acc to an industry expert, Coherent sources ~40% of its materials externally.

Mgmt attributed a big part of the breakout quarter to new products launched last year. They introduced two, but they consistently flag one: a new ITO formulation.

text:
As data center demands accelerate, the future of AI will depend not only on processing power, but on how efficiently systems are connected.

@robpegoraro  explores how photonics can address the growing pressure for AI data centers, and what role optical networking plays in the connectivity stack of the AI infrastructure.

Read more on @PCMag , featuring Taara CEO Mahesh Krishnaswamy, NTT, and @nttdata_inc's #IOWN: 

https://t.co/V4xtkT2KoD
@crux_capital_ I agree, but my main worry with photonics is that its pullback isn’t necessarily a new base. It’s trading back to where it was pre ATH

text:
Before turning to photonics, I tried my luck in biotech. The field caught my interest quite strongly, and for about two years I read almost exclusively books in that area. Overall, I had a fairly good track record.

I don’t own any of the four stocks $QURE, $CMPS, $CAPR, or https://t.co/zgqEYAzfFg, anymore. Back then, all four were asymmetric bets for me, and contrary to the odds, three of them worked out very well (multibaggers), while even the fourth still ended up slightly positive.

A few weeks ago, I made another biotech investment where I see an asymmetric opportunity. Unfortunately, I can’t share it here, even though I’ve written a full write-up on it. The company is simply too small, and sharing it would likely drive the price up significantly. Ideally, it should re-rate for fundamental reasons, not because of attention or momentum. In cases like this, a paid Substack model would actually make sense.

Once the case plays out, either successfully or not, I’ll share it here. That way it will be documented whether my “biotech touch” is still intact.

Many people probably associate me primarily with photonics, but in reality I’m more of a generalist covering a range of different fields. I try to avoid having only a single hammer in the toolbox, otherwise every problem starts looking like a nail.
embedded_text: Bought $QURE last week. The FDA must be crazy not to approve their candidate for Huntington's disease. Together with $CAPR, $CMPS and $CMP.AX, these are four companies with very promising data that are very likely to receive approval within the next 1-2 years.
